contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The relationship between [[class]]es (blueprints) and objects (instances)
- Defining fields (data) and [[method]]s (behavior)
- Creating objects using the new operator

These are the most fundamental concepts in object-oriented programming.

contents.title: Defining a Class and Creating Instances
contents.body: Define a class and create an **instance** using the new keyword.
contents.note: You can create any number of **instances** from a single class. Each [[instance]] holds its own independent field values.

contents.title: Components of a Class
contents.body:
The four elements that make up a class:

1. Fields: [[Variable]]s that hold data
- **Instance** variables: values owned by each object
- Class variables (static): values shared across the entire class

2. Methods: define behavior and operations
- Instance methods: operations on an object
- Class methods (static): operations on the class

3. **Constructors**: initialization logic
   - Called automatically when new is used

4. Nested classes: classes defined inside a class (advanced)

Default values for fields:
| Type | Default Value |
|---|---|
| int, long | 0 |
| double, float | 0.0 |
| boolean | false |
| Reference types | null |
*Note: Local variables have no default values!
contents.note: Fields automatically receive default values when declared, but local variables cause a [[compile]] error if not initialized.

contents.title: Visualizing the Relationship Between Classes and Objects
contents.body:
Think of it like a "cookie cutter" and "cookies."

| Concept | Analogy | In Code |
|---|---|---|
| Class | The cookie "cutter" | Blueprint (only one) |
| Object | The baked "cookie" | Instance (can make many) |
| Field | Flavor, size | Data |
| Method | Eat, reheat | Behavior/operations |

You can make as many cookies (objects) as you want from a single cutter (class).
contents.note: Every time you write new Car(), a new Car object is created.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Class = blueprint, **Instance** = actual object
- Create instances with the new keyword
- Fields: data, Methods: behavior
- Fields have default values (local variables do not)
- You can create multiple instances from a single class

quizzes.question: Which keyword is used to create an instance from a class?
quizzes.explanation: In Java, you create an instance of a class using the new keyword. Writing new Car() creates an object in memory based on the Car class blueprint.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The default value for an int field is 0. Even though the count field in Counter is not explicitly initialized, it is a field, so it receives the default value of 0.

quizzes.question:
Fill in the blank to create an instance of a class.
Dog d = _____ Dog();
quizzes.explanation: new Dog() creates an instance of the Dog class. The new keyword allocates an object in heap memory and invokes the constructor.

lessons.title: The this Keyword and Field Operations

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Using the this keyword to refer to "the current object itself"
- How to distinguish between fields and local [[variable]]s with the same name
- Initializing and manipulating fields

Mastering this makes the intent of your code much clearer.

contents.title: How to Use this
contents.body: Use this to distinguish between fields and local variables.
contents.note: this.fieldName means "this object's own field." If the parameter and field names are different, this can be omitted.

contents.title: Two Uses of this
contents.body:
The this keyword has two primary uses.

| Usage | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Field reference | this.name = name | Distinguish from a local variable with the same name |
| Constructor call | this(args) | Call another constructor in the same class |

this() must be placed on the first line of a [[constructor]].
contents.note: this() and super() cannot be used together.

contents.title: Field Initialization and null
contents.body: Handling [[reference type]] fields and preventing [[NullPointerException]].
contents.note: Fields can be given initial values at declaration time. They can also be overwritten in a **constructor**.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- this = a reference to the current **instance** itself
- Use this.fieldName to distinguish a field from a parameter
- Without this, parameters (local variables) take priority
- The default value for reference type fields is null
- Calling a [[method]] on a null reference → NullPointerException
- Fields can be given initial values at declaration

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: In the set() method, value = value simply assigns the parameter value to itself — the field is not changed. It should be written as this.value = value. The field retains its default value of 0.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes the this keyword?
quizzes.explanation: this is a reference to the current instance itself. It is primarily used to distinguish between fields and local variables (parameters) that share the same name. this cannot be used inside a static method because no instance exists in that context.

choices.text: Can be used inside static methods

quizzes.question: What is the value when accessing an uninitialized String field?
quizzes.explanation: When a reference type field is not initialized, its default value is null. This applies only to instance variables (fields) — local variables will cause a compile error if not initialized.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Defining **constructors** and when they are called
- Initializing objects with parameterized [[constructor]]s
- Constructor [[overloading]] and this() calls

You'll learn the mechanism for "always initializing objects when they are created" using constructors.

contents.title: Defining a Constructor
contents.body: A constructor has the same name as the [[class]] and does not have a return type.
contents.note: If you do not define any **constructors**, Java automatically generates a no-argument default constructor. Once you define even one constructor, the default is no longer generated.

contents.title: Constructor Overloading and this()
contents.body: Define multiple **constructors** with different parameters and call another constructor using this().
contents.note: this() can only be used on the first line of a **constructor**. It cannot be used together with super().

contents.title: Special Rules for Constructors
contents.body:
**Constructors** have different rules from regular [[method]]s.

| Rule | Description |
|---|---|
| Name matches the class name | For a Car class, it's Car() |
| No return type specified | Don't even write void |
| Called only with new | Cannot be called directly |
| Auto-generated if none defined | A no-argument default constructor |

Note: If you define a parameterized constructor, the default constructor is no longer auto-generated.
contents.note: On exams, questions about "conditions under which the default **constructor** is or is not auto-generated" are very common.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Constructor** = same name as the class, no return type
- Automatically called with new to initialize the object
- If none are defined, a default constructor is auto-generated
- Defining even one constructor removes the default constructor
- Multiple constructors with different parameters via overloading
- Use this() to call another constructor in the same class (first line only)

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes a constructor?
quizzes.explanation: A constructor has the same name as the class and does not specify a return type (not even void). It is automatically executed when an instance is created with new, and it initializes the object.

choices.text: Its return type is specified as void
It has the same name as the class and no return type
It can be called at any time

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: new Msg() calls the no-argument constructor, which calls this("default"), invoking the parameterized constructor. Since this.text = "default" is set, "default" is output.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: Since the Item class defines a parameterized constructor Item(String), the default constructor Item() is not auto-generated. Line 7's new Item() tries to call a no-argument constructor that doesn't exist, causing a compile error.

quizzes.question: Complete the constructor for the Student class to initialize the name field.
quizzes.explanation: In a constructor, you use the this keyword to reference the current class's field and assign the parameter value to initialize it. The constructor name is the same as the class name, and no return type is specified.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- **Encapsulation**: a design technique for hiding data from outside access
- The differences between **access modifiers** (public, private, protected, default)
- Safe data access through getter/setter methods

[[Encapsulation]] is a critical technique for "preventing bugs and writing code that is resilient to change."

contents.title: private and getter/setter
contents.body: Make fields private and control access through public methods.
contents.note: By making the balance field private, you can completely prevent the balance from going negative.

contents.title: The Four Access Modifiers
contents.body:
Java has four levels of **access modifiers**:

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass | Other Packages |
|--------|---|---|---|---|
| private | ○ | × | × | × |
| (none) package-private | ○ | ○ | × | × |
| protected | ○ | ○ | ○ | × |
| public | ○ | ○ | ○ | ○ |

Guidelines for choosing:

| Target | Recommended Level |
|---|---|
| Fields | private (encapsulation) |
| getter/setter | public |
| Internal utility methods | private |
| Methods for subclass use | protected |
| API public methods | public |

Principle: Choose the minimum access level needed (principle of information hiding).
contents.note: Only public and (none) can be used for top-level [[class]]es. private and protected can only be used for nested classes.

contents.title: Choosing the Right Access Modifier
contents.body:
Review all four **access modifiers** at a glance.

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass | Anywhere |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| private | ○ | × | × | × |
| (none) | ○ | ○ | × | × |
| protected | ○ | ○ | ○ | × |
| public | ○ | ○ | ○ | ○ |

Basic approach: Make fields private, and choose the narrowest modifier needed for methods.
contents.note: "(none)" is called default access or package-private.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **Encapsulation**: protect fields with private + getter/setter
- Add validation (value checking) in setters
- Four levels: private < (none) < protected < public
- Principle: choose the minimum access level
- Fields should generally be private; methods should be chosen based on purpose
contents.note: **[Practical Tips]**In practice, **encapsulation** is essential when designing DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) and Entities (database-mapped classes). Using the Lombok library to auto-generate getters/setters is the standard approach in the industry.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: The code field is private, so it cannot be accessed from outside the Secret class. Line 7's s.code causes a compile error. A getter method would need to be provided to access it.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a benefit of encapsulation?
quizzes.explanation: Encapsulation restricts direct access to fields and uses setters with validation (value checking) to prevent invalid values from being set. Changes to the internal implementation are minimized in their impact on external code.

quizzes.question: Which access modifier allows access from within the same package and from subclasses?
quizzes.explanation: protected allows access from classes within the same package and from subclasses even in different packages. private allows access from the same class only, package-private (no modifier) from the same package only, and public from everywhere.

quizzes.question: Arrange the steps from object creation to method invocation in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: First, create an object with new, set values with a setter, operate with a method, and retrieve the result with a getter.

lessons.title: static Members
lessons.summary: Data and methods shared across the entire class

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[static field]]s: [[variable]]s shared across all **instances**
- [[static method]]s: [[method]]s callable without an [[instance]]
- When to use static and important caveats

Math.random() and Integer.parseInt() are examples of static methods.

contents.title: static Fields and Methods
contents.body: static members exist as a single copy across the entire [[class]].
contents.note: A static field is created only once when the class is loaded. It remains in memory until the JVM terminates.

contents.title: static Restrictions and Usage Guidelines
contents.body:
Restrictions on static members:

1. A static method cannot directly access **instance** members
   → You must create an object first to access them

2. this cannot be used in a static method
   → Because this refers to "the current instance"

3. Instance methods can access static members

Appropriate uses of static:
| Use Case | Description |
|---|---|
| Constants | static final double PI = 3.14159; |
| Utility methods | Math.max(), Integer.parseInt() |
| Counters | Managing instance counts |
| Factory methods | Providing instance creation methods |

Uses to avoid:
- Static fields with lots of state (causes issues in testing and multi-[[thread]]ing)
- Making everything static (loses the benefits of object-oriented programming)

contents.title: static Methods Cannot Access Instance Members
contents.body:
Because static methods "belong to the class," they cannot access information of a specific **instance**.

- this cannot be used inside a static method
- A static method cannot directly reference instance fields
- A static method cannot directly call instance methods

Conversely, instance methods can access static members.
contents.note: The [[main method]] is also static, which is why you can't directly call **instance** methods from main — this is because of this rule.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- static = belongs to the class (no **instance** required)
- static field: a value shared across all instances
- static method: called with ClassName.methodName()
- Cannot directly access instance members from static
- Accessing static from an instance is allowed
- static final is used for defining constants

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The static field count is shared across all instances. Calling new Counter() three times executes count++ in the constructor three times, so count becomes 3.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: The static method show() tries to directly access the instance field value. Since a static method does not belong to any instance, direct access to instance members causes a compile error. The error occurs on line 4.

quizzes.question: What is the combination of static final used for?
quizzes.explanation: static final is used to define class-level constants. static makes it belong to the class, and final makes it immutable. Examples include Math.PI and Integer.MAX_VALUE. The naming convention is uppercase letters with underscores (MAX_VALUE).

quizzes.question: Select all correct benefits of encapsulation.
quizzes.explanation: The benefits of encapsulation are: "internal implementation is hidden, making code resilient to change," "invalid values can be prevented," and "only the necessary interface is exposed to class users." Improved processing speed is not a direct benefit of encapsulation.

choices.text: Changes to internal implementation have minimal impact on external code
Only the necessary interface can be exposed

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- toString(): customizing the string representation of an object
- equals(): defining logical equality comparison
- hashCode(): required for hash-based collections

Let's properly [[override]] the methods of the Object class that all [[class]]es implicitly have.

contents.title: Overriding toString()
contents.body: Customize the string representation of an object.
contents.note: toString() is extremely useful for debugging and log output. Make it a habit to override it in every class.

contents.title: equals() and hashCode()
contents.body: An implementation for correctly determining object equality.
contents.note: If you override equals(), you must also override hashCode(). For [[HashMap]]/[[HashSet]] to work correctly, there is a contract that objects for which equals returns true must return the same hashCode.

contents.title: The Difference Between == and equals()
contents.body:
- == [[operator]]
  → [[Primitive type]]s: compares values
  → [[Reference type]]s: compares references (memory addresses)
  → Determines whether two variables point to the same object

- equals() method
  → Can only be used with reference types
  → Without overriding, behaves the same as ==
  → When overridden, determines "logical equality"
  → The String class already overrides equals()

Note: String comparison
  [[String]] s1 = "hello";
  String s2 = "hello";
  s1 == s2;      // true (same reference due to String pool)
  
  String s3 = new String("hello");
  s1 == s3;      // false (different objects)
  s1.equals(s3); // true (same content)
  
→ Always use equals() for string comparison

contents.title: Always Override hashCode When You Override equals
contents.body:
An important contract in Java:

- Two objects where equals() returns true → hashCode() must return the same value
- If hashCode() differs → equals() must return false

Breaking this contract will cause HashSet and HashMap to malfunction.
contents.note: By using your IDE's auto-generation feature, you can correctly generate equals() and hashCode() as a pair.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- All classes implicitly [[inherit]] from Object
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| toString() | String representation, auto-called by println |
| equals() | Logical equality comparison |
| hashCode() | Must be overridden together with equals() |
- == compares references, equals() compares content
- Always use equals() for string comparison

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Strings created with new String() are separate objects, so a == b is false (different references). a.equals(b) compares the content, so it returns true (both are "Java").

quizzes.question: Which method should also be overridden when overriding equals()?
quizzes.explanation: According to the Object class contract, two objects for which equals() returns true must return the same hashCode(). This is necessary for HashMap and HashSet to work correctly. Overriding only equals() without hashCode() will cause unexpected behavior in collections.

quizzes.question: What is displayed when printing an object of a class that hasn't overridden toString()?
quizzes.explanation: The default toString() from the Object class returns "class name @ hexadecimal hash code." For example, it would be something like MyClass@1a2b3c4d. To display meaningful information, you need to override toString().

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Organizing [[class]]es and avoiding name collisions with packages
- How to write import statements and important caveats
- Three uses of the final keyword ([[variable]]s, [[method]]s, and classes)

This is essential knowledge as your projects grow larger.

contents.title: Packages and import
contents.body: Organize classes with packages and use classes from other packages with import.
contents.note: Classes in the java.lang package ([[String]], Math, Integer, etc.) are automatically imported. You don't need to write import statements for them.

contents.title: Three Uses of final
contents.body:
The final keyword has different meanings depending on where it's used.

| Target | Meaning | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| final variable | Reassignment prohibited | Value can only be set once |
| final method | Override prohibited | Cannot be overridden in subclasses |
| final class | Inheritance prohibited | Cannot create subclasses (e.g.: String) |

Why the String class is final: to guarantee security and immutability.

contents.title: The final Keyword
contents.body: final is a modifier meaning "modification prohibited."
contents.note: The String class is final to guarantee security and immutability. If subclasses could modify equals() or hashCode(), it would cause problems.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- package: organizes classes into categories (corresponds to folder structure)
- import: declares usage of classes from other packages
- java.lang is auto-imported (String, Math, etc.)
| Target | Description |
|---|---|
| final variable | Reassignment prohibited (for reference types, modifying contents is allowed) |
| final method | Override prohibited |
| final class | Inheritance prohibited (String, Integer, etc.) |

quizzes.question: Which class can be used without an import statement?
quizzes.explanation: Classes in the java.lang package are automatically imported, so no explicit import statement is needed. String, Math, System, Integer, Object, etc. all belong to java.lang. ArrayList is in the java.util package and requires an import.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes a compile error?
quizzes.explanation: x is a final variable, so it cannot be reassigned. Line 4's x = 20 causes a compile error. On the other hand, line 3's list.add("hello") modifies the contents of the list, not the reference itself, so it is allowed.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes a final class?
quizzes.explanation: A final class cannot be inherited (extended) by other classes. The String class is final to guarantee immutability and security. All methods in a final class are implicitly final.

choices.text: It cannot be inherited by other classes
